coredata.dc:title: Detection of picric acid in industrial effluents using multifunctional green fluorescent B/N-carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: P, Keerthana
Cherian, Anila Rose
Sirimahachai, Uraiwan
Thadathil, Ditto Abraham
Varghese, Anitha
Hegde, Gurumurthy
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots have recently gained widespread attention due to their excellent physicochemical features. The rapid escalation in the dumping of hazardous chemicals into water, spurred demand for developing efficient and selective sensors for toxic chemicals. Herein, we have developed a novel fluorescence sensor for picric acid which is a major pollutant in industrial effluents. The new strategy exploits the development of a fluorescence sensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) followed by boron functionalization. The N-CQDs were synthesized in a rapid single-step microwave technique by employing L-serine and citric acid. Subsequent boron functionalization of N-CQDs was carried out using boric acid for the synthesis of Boron-nitrogen carbon quantum dots (B/N-CQDs). The B/N-CQDs were found to exhibit high quantum yield (24%), good water solubility, outstanding photostability features, and bright green fluorescence under UV light. The morphology of B/N-CQDs is spherical, with scattered particle sizes ranging from 2 to 8 nanometers. Furthermore, B/N-CQDs were found to be an effective fluorescence probe for the selective and sensitive detection of picric acid, with a good linear range of 37 nM-30 µM and a detection limit of 1.8 nM. The Photoluminescence (PL) intensity of B/N-CQDs was selectively quenched by picric acid. The quenching mechanism was conclusively established using fluorescence lifetime decay studies. Moreover, the synthesized B/N-CQDs was successfully employed for the analysis of picric acid from industrial effluents and cell imaging with Hela cells to showcase the utility of the developed fluorescent probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron/nitrogen co-doped
Carbon quantum dots
Picric acid
Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET)
Vitro cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of B/N-CQDs by functionalization of N-CQDs using boric acid
Cytotoxicity measurement and life cell imaging
Cell culture and cell viability assay
Study on photophysical properties of B/N-CQDs
Determination of B/N-CQD photoluminescence stability and its application thereof in the analysis of picric acid
Selectivity study for the detection of toxic picric acid
Determination of the sensitivity of B/N-CQDs in selective detection of PA
Analysis of picric acid in industrial effluent water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The researchers and authors express their gratitude to the Major Research Project (Major Research Project DSC-1830 ), Center for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka, India for providing the required financial support for completing this research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Figure 1(a) TEM image of B/N-CQDs. (b) Histogram particle size distribution of B/N-CQDs.
Figure 2(a) XRD pattern of N-CQDs, B/N-CQDs, and BA. (b) Raman spectrum of B/N-CQDs.
Figure 3FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs and B/N-CQDs.

Figure 4XPS spectrum of N-CQDs (a) Survey spectra. (b)C1s spectrum. (c) N1s spectrum. (d) O1s spectrum; XPS spectrum of B/N-CQDs (e) survey spectra. (f) C1s spectrum. (g) B1s spectrum. (h) N1s spectrum. (i) O1s spectrum.

Figure 5(a) Absorption spectra of B/N-CQDs (100 μL) and B/N-CQDs (100 μL) + (2 μL /16 μM) P.A (Inset: optical images in daylight (left) and UV light (right)). (b) Excitation and emission spectra of B/N-CQDs.
Figure 6(a) Excitation dependent emission spectra of B/N-CQDs (b) Area normalized excitation dependent emission spectra of B/N-CQDs.

Figure 8(a) Selectivity of PA detection towards various other pollutants. (b) Different concentrations of PA ranging up to 30 μM. (c) Linear plot between PL intensity and concentrations of Picric acid (0–30 μM).

Figure 9(a) Spectral overlap between emission spectra of B/N-CQDs and absorption spectra of PA. (b) Stern-Volmer plot for B/N-CQDs on the addition of PA (c) Modified Stern-Volmer plot for B/N-CQDs on the addition of PA. (d) PL decay in the presence and absence of PA.

Figure 10(a) Cell viability assay of Hela cells incubated with different concentrations of B/N-CQDs after 24 hrs. (b) Confocal images of Hela cells incubated with different concentrations of B/N-CQDs.
Scheme 1Schematic representation for the synthesis of B/N-CQD.
Scheme 2Schematic illustration for the detection of picric acid by B/N-CQD.
Comparison of Proposed work with the other existing literature on picric acid determination.

row.entry: L-serine, citric acid and boric acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of picric acid in various industrial effluents.

head.ce:title: Detection of picric acid in industrial effluents using multifunctional green fluorescent B/N-carbon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots have recently gained widespread attention due to their excellent physicochemical features. The rapid escalation in the dumping of hazardous chemicals into water, spurred demand for developing efficient and selective sensors for toxic chemicals. Herein, we have developed a novel fluorescence sensor for picric acid which is a major pollutant in industrial effluents. The new strategy exploits the development of a fluorescence sensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) followed by boron functionalization. The N-CQDs were synthesized in a rapid single-step microwave technique by employing L-serine and citric acid. Subsequent boron functionalization of N-CQDs was carried out using boric acid for the synthesis of Boron-nitrogen carbon quantum dots (B/N-CQDs). The B/N-CQDs were found to exhibit high quantum yield (24%), good water solubility, outstanding photostability features, and bright green fluorescence under UV light. The morphology of B/N-CQDs is spherical, with scattered particle sizes ranging from 2 to 8 nanometers. Furthermore, B/N-CQDs were found to be an effective fluorescence probe for the selective and sensitive detection of picric acid, with a good linear range of 37 nM-30 µM and a detection limit of 1.8 nM. The Photoluminescence (PL) intensity of B/N-CQDs was selectively quenched by picric acid. The quenching mechanism was conclusively established using fluorescence lifetime decay studies. Moreover, the synthesized B/N-CQDs was successfully employed for the analysis of picric acid from industrial effluents and cell imaging with Hela cells to showcase the utility of the developed fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:para: Even at low quantities, environmental pollution has become a severe and critical concern in the globe today. Organic pollutants (OPs) have a long retention time in the environment, high photostability, low biodegradability, and high toxicity. Furthermore, substituted phenols and their derivatives are the most common OPs, which are widely employed in a variety of industries including food, cosmetics, medicines, insecticides, and photography. These moieties lead to serious health concerns, such as birth control troubles, hormone imbalances, drowsiness, chronic headaches, and cyanosis [1,2]. The successful elimination of these phenols is unavoidable due to snow bowling effect attributed by its toxicity [3].

The major environmental contaminant, nitroaromatic compounds is detrimental to human health due to their highly explosive and toxic nature. Trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), and trinitrophenol (TNP) are the most regularly employed nitroaromatic explosives [4,5]. Among the nitroaromatic compounds, TNP known as picric acid (PA) have a higher explosive ability than TNT [6,7]. The high acidity and low biodegradability of PA are attributable to the electron-withdrawing nitro groups, contributing for the ground-water and soil pollution [8]. The industries which contribute to this pollution include leather, pharmaceutical, fireworks, glass, chemicals, and dye. PA consumption can result in skin irritation, eye itching, irritation, liver disease, and a variety of chronic disorders such as anaemia, cancer, and cyanosis issue [9]. The biotransformation result of PA was found to be highly toxic compared to its corresponding parent compound [10]. In China, “Drinking water health standard (GB5749–2006)” and “surface water environmental quality standard (GB3838–2002)” modulate that the acceptable concentration of PA should be less than 0.5 mg/L in water [8]. So, the development of fast, convenient, selective, and sensitive analytical methods for the detection of PA is highly desirable.

As a result of their structural similarities, detection of the nitroaromatic compounds is a tedious process. Irrespective of the fact several analytical techniques have been employed for its detection such as electrochemical sensing [11], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [13], mass spectrometry [14], atomic absorption spectroscopy [15], ion chromatography [16], fluorescence methods garnered more attention than other analytical methods due to their great selectivity, sensitivity, fast response, and portability. The aforementioned approaches necessitate the use of complex instruments, time-consuming preparation, and significant costs. Carbon quantum dots made a serendipous entry to the carbon materials, showcased good biocompatibility, great photobleaching resistance, quick synthesis time, and minimal toxicity when compared to other fluorophores [17,18] such as coumarin, pyrene, xanthene derivatives, and metallic organic frameworks. Carbon quantum dots are an unusual type of carbon that has a quasi-spherical shape, is amorphous, contains sp2/sp3 carbon domain, and emits brilliant blue and green fluorescence when exposed to UV light.

Carbon quantum dots have been synthesized by various methods namely pyrolysis [19], laser ablation [20,21], hydrothermal treatment [22–26], chemical oxidation [27] and microwave irradiation method [28]. In comparison to the microwave irradiation method, other techniques require a longer time for synthesis, sophisticated instruments, and intense acid treatment which makes the former advantageous. The development of various functionalized CQDs in the sensing of picric acid has been reported in the recent years. Qingyuan et.al., developed amine-capped quantum dots for the detection of PA with low quantum yield (13%) and high detection limit value (1 μM) [29]. Xiang et.al., synthesized dots by a microwave-assisted method using a single precursor, ammonium citrate dibasic yielding a quantum yield of 20% with a linear range of 0–100 μM [30]. Cong et al. synthesized screening-based functional quantum dots to detect enzyme activity with a detection limit of 0.6 μL [31]. Ju et.al., prepared yellow-green carbon dots for detecting explosive 2,4,6- TNP which had a low quantum yield (13.2%) and LOD was found to be 2 μM [32]. Subhash et.al., reported a biocompatible material of N/S co-doped quantum dots synthesized by hydrothermal method to detect picric acid applicable in real-life cell imaging with a linear range of 0–200 μM [33]. CQDs doped with heteroatoms and subjected to surface passivation can improve quantum yield and fluorescence capabilities in quantum dots. It is highly desirable to have an effective sensor with higher quantum yield, sensitivity, and selectivity for detecting picric acid.

Herein we have developed a novel fluorescence sensor for picric acid which is a major pollutant in industrial effluents. The new strategy exploits the development of a fluorescence sensor based on N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) followed by boron functionalization with L-Serine and Citric acid in a microwave reactor ( Scheme 1). The developed B/N-CQDs could achieve good photoluminescence quantum yields and be used as an efficient fluorescence probe for a highly selective and sensitive method for detecting picric acid industrial effluents.

Picric acid (PA), citric acid, L-serine, boric acid, anthracene, dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4) and monopotassium phosphate (K2HPO4) were purchased from SD Fine-Chem Pvt Ltd., India. All the chemicals procured were of pure analytical grade. Deionized water was employed for the preparation of all solutions throughout the experiment.

FT-IR spectrum was employed to determine the surface group functionalities of both quantum dots surfaces and was carried out using Thermo Nicolet, Avatar 370. JEOL (JEM-F200) electron microscope was used to record TEM images at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Rigaku Smart Lab X-ray diffractometer was used to determine Powder XRD patterns using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Ǻ). Lab RAM HR FT-Raman module, was used to record Raman spectrum at 532 nm LASER. ESCA-14 (Omicron Nanotechnology) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was used to record XPS spectrum. Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–visible 1800) was used for absorption measurements. All fluorescence measurements (excitation, emission, contour map, PL) were done by using Shimadzu spectrofluorometer (RF5301PC). All measurements were recorded by using PBS at pH 7 medium.

The N-CQDs were prepared by an environmentally benign microwave-assisted method. 0.275 g of L-serine and 0.75 g of citric acid were dissolved in 5 mL deionized water with continuous stirring. After ultrasonication for 10 mins, the obtained solution was heated at 600 W for 3 mins in a microwave reactor. The solution was cooled down to normal temperature and dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water with stirring to get a brown solution. Finally, the resultant solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 12 mins to separate the impurity and filtered using a Whatman filter. The acquired solution was finally extracted using ethyl acetate and stored in a refrigerator under 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of B/N-CQDs by functionalization of N-CQDs using boric acid
ce:section.ce:para: A known amount of N-CQDs (0.1 mg) was dissolved in 5 mL of double-distilled water. After adding boric acid (0.124 g), the solution was kept under reflux condition at 60 °C for 24 h. The obtained solution was cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, the brown residue was obtained by adding 2-propanol and collected as B/N-CQDs after subjecting to high-speed centrifugation. The fluorescence stability of the synthesized B/N-CQDs was investigated using different ionic strength (0–2 M KCl), pH (1−14), UV irradiation over a period of time, and storage stability. The excitation spectra were observed at 335 nm using a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The PL quenching studies were carried out at room temperature using B/N-CQDs by the standard addition method. Aliquots of solution containing varying concentrations of PA (37 nM- 30 μM) were transferred into 3 mL cuvettes. To this 100 μL of B/N-CQDs (0.1 mg/mL), 400 μL of 0.1 M Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7) were added and diluted upto 3 mL using deionized water. The fluorescence excitation and emission intensity were subsequently monitored at 340 nm and 427 nm respectively. A calibration graph was constructed by plotting concentration of PA against fluorescence intensity. This calibration graph was used for the analysis of picric acid in industrial effluent samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity measurement and life cell imaging
Cell culture and cell viability assay
ce:section.ce:para:
HeLa cells are an established and well-characterized cell line that has demonstrated reproducible results with high proliferative growth. The culture medium for Hela cancer cells inhibits minimal essential medium (MEM) supplemented with fetal bovine serum (10%) in a standard incubator at 37 °C consisting of 5% CO2. HeLa cell line was cultured by a suitable protocol. In contrast, HeLa cells were placed in a 96-well plated culture medium with a concentration of 1 × 105 cell per well and incubated with 0, 25, 50, 75, 100 μg concentrations of B/N-CQDs for 24 hrs with MTT(3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay at 37 °C with 5% CO2. At the end of incubation, the MTT solution prepared in PBS was added to every well plate and incubated for 3 hrs at 37 °C. Finally, MTT solution in the culture medium was removed and DMSO was added to solvate the formazan crystals. The obtained formazan crystals were observed at an excitation wavelength of 570 nm. The cell viability percentage can be calculated by using the absorbance value with the following formula,
(1)
%
of
cell
viability
=
Absorbance
570
nm
of
treated
cells
Absorbance
570
nm
of
controlled
cells
× 100

HeLa cells (1 ×104) were placed in a 6-well plated culture medium and incubated overnight. The different concentrations of B/N-CQDs were added to the culture medium, which was incubated at 37 °C for 24 hrs with 5% CO2. The cells were placed in a slide for examination and imaged using a confocal microscope. The confocal microscope images were absorbed in an excitation wavelength of 570 nm and the images were captured using a fluorescence microscope.

The PL quantum yield of the synthesized B/N-CQDs, was calculated by using anthracene as a reference. B/N-CQDs were dissolved in deionized water, whereas anthracene (Φ = 0.27%) was dissolved in ethanol. In order to minimize the reabsorption effect, absorbance was maintained under 0.1. B/N-CQDs quantum yield was determined by the following equation [34].
(2)
∅
B / N − CQD
=
∅
Anthracene
×
I
B / N − CQD
I
Anthracene
×
ŋ 2
B / N − CQD
ŋ 2
Anthracene
×
A
Anthracene
A
B / N − CQD
Where ØB/N-CQD is the quantum yield of the sample and Øanthracene is the quantum yield of the reference, IB/N-CQD and IAnthracene is the integrated emission intensity of sample and reference, A is the absorbance recorded at 346 nm, and ƞ is the refractive index.

The preparation of B/N-CQDs was governed via microwave irradiation for 3 min at 600 W using L-Serine, Citric acid followed by boron functionalization with boric acid (as shown in Scheme 1). TEM analysis was carried out to study the particle size and shape. The TEM images of the as-prepared B/N-CQDs indicate quasi-spherical morphology of the quantum dots, as shown in Fig. 1(a). The diameter of boron/nitrogen-doped quantum dots has narrow size distribution in the range of 2–8 nm. The average diameter for B/N-CQDs was determined to be 4.5 nm. Fig. 1(b) represents the histogram for the average distribution of the particle size over 100 nanoparticles. The TEM images explain the monodispersity of the synthesized B/N-CQDs.

The XRD pattern of N-CQDs and B/N-CQDs are represented in Fig. 2(a). The XRD pattern confirms a broad single peak at around 2θ = 22.54, which corresponds to (002) plane having interlaying space distance at 0.39 nm for N-CQDs. The XRD pattern for B/N-CQDs has a broad small hump at around 2θ = 22.01, which corresponds to the 002 planes having an interlaying distance of 0.40 nm while the other peaks at 100, 110, 111 plane are characteristic peaks of boron oxide (B2O3). The peaks confirm the carbon quantum dots are highly amorphous with the sp3 domain. The interlayer distance was calculated by using Bragg’s equation given below,
(3)
d = n λ / 2 sin θ
d is the interplanar spacing, θ is the peak position, n is the diffraction order, and λ is the Incident ray wavelength.

The Raman spectrum of boron-nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots as seen from Fig. 2(b) have two peaks positioned at 1362.76 and 1520.23 cm−1, that corresponds to D and G bands, respectively [35]. The D band (A1 g) clearly explains the carbon atom vibration in the termination plane with dangling bonds due to disordered glassy carbon or graphite. The G band (E2 g) mode explains the graphitic peak related to the stretching vibration of C-C bonded atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) lattice. The extent of defect of B/N-CQDs was determined on analysing the peak position and the intensity of the D and G bands (ID/IG). The calculated ID/IG ratio is 1.01, which is the characteristic disorder extent of the sp3/sp2 carbon domain.

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to obtain the surface functional groups present in carbon quantum dots, this was in well accordance with the literature [36]. Comparison of FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs with B/N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 3. A broad absorption band was observed for N-CQDs at 3421 cm−1 which corresponds to the N-H and O-H stretching vibration, whereas another evidence for amino groups such as C-N is present at 1164 cm−1. The bands at 2927 cm−1 and 2848 cm−1 were indicative to the CH3 and CH2 stretching vibration. The small bands at 1448 cm−1 (asymmetric) and 1393 cm−1 (symmetric) correspond to C-H bending vibration respectively. The sharp band at 1692 cm−1, was attributed to CO carbonyl stretching vibration, whereas the bands at 1040 cm−1 and 1343 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C-O and COO- band respectively. The less intense band at 1563 cm−1 is indicative of CC stretching vibration merged with the carbonyl peak.

The B/N-CQDs showed a sharp band at 1577 cm−1 which corresponds to B2O3 bending vibrations. The bands observed at 1408 cm−1 and 1102 cm−1 were attributed to B-O vibration and B-O bond stretching at the trigonal unit [37]. The small band observed at 906 cm−1 is suggestive of the B-C stretching vibration, respectively whereas the remaining bands are similar to N/CQDs devoid of boron functionalization.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is one of the quantitative techniques to determine the elemental state, composition and binding energy of the material of interest. XPS measured the ratio of different element core level peaks, whereas the peaks were deconvoluted by oxidation states [38]. The shift of binding energy is due to the heteroatoms associated with the carbon moiety such as nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur and halogens [39].

The confirmation of boron functionalization in nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots was quantified using XPS analysis of N/CQDs and B-N/CQDs. The survey spectra of N-CQDs shown three distinct characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s, and O1s, which have binding energies 285, 400, 532 eV shown in Fig. 4(a), respectively. The core-level spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s region of N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 4(b, c, and d) respectively. The percentage of the elements C, O, and N were 76%, 18.32%, and 2%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum core level of C1s in Fig. 4(b) shows an intense characteristic peak at 284.821 eV attributed to the C-C/CC bond. The less intense peak at 285.91 eV is indicative of the C-N bond. The peak represents the presence of the C-O bond due to the carbonyl group, which was observed at 286.93 eV. Another evidence for the presence of carbonyl group CO shows an intense peak at 289.91 eV. A broad spectrum of sp2 carbon domain featured π-π * satellite peak at 286.8 eV. The core-level spectrum of N1s in Fig. 4(c) shows two characteristic peaks, which indicates C-N and N-H respectively [40]. The sharp intense peak at 400.46 eV represents the C-N bond, and another peak obtained at 401.75 eV, corresponds to the N-H bond. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s Fig. 4(d) deconvoluted into four peaks at 531.4, 532.30, 533.9, and 534.5 eV. The intense peak at 531.4 eV, corresponds to the CO bond owing to the presence of the carboxyl group on the surface of N-CQD. The intense peak at 532.30 eV corresponds to C-O bond. The peak at 534.5 eV is attributed to the COO- bond owing to the presence of carboxyl moiety at the surface. The peak at 533.9 eV belongs to the C-O-C/C-O-H bond presence of hydroxyl or carbonyl group respectively [41].

Post-treatment of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with boric acid yielded boron-nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots. In addition to O1s, C1s, N1s, B/N-CQDs have B1s present at the surface layer of the B/N-CQDs. The survey spectrum of B/N-CQDs Fig. 4(e) shown four distinct characteristic peaks that have binding energies 285, 401, 533, and 194 eV which are relevant to C1s, N1s, O1s, and B1s respectively. The elemental composition percentage of C, N, O, B are 40.61%, 3.46%, 37.89%, and 18.04% respectively. Fig. 4(f) depicts the high-resolution spectrum of C1s which has five characteristic peaks 284.16, 289.06, 286.2, 285.07, 291.4 eV due to the presence of CC, CO, C-O, C-N, and π-π * satellite peak (sp2 domain) bonds respectively. The core level B1s XPS spectrum as shown in Fig. 4(g) has three intense characteristic peaks at 194.55, 193.5, and 192.8 eV indicative of B-O, B2O3 and BCO2 bond respectively [37]. The core level spectrum of N1s as shown in Fig. 4(h) has two distinct characteristic peaks at 400.5 and 401.24 eV due to the presence of C-N and N-H bonds respectively. The high-resolution core level O1s spectrum as shown in Fig. 4(i) has five distinct characteristic peaks. The peak at 531.6 eV corresponds to B-O/CO bonds. The intense characteristic peak at 533.2 eV is due to the trigonal boron-oxygen bond in B2O3. The peaks at 532.4, 533.95, 534.6 eV are indicative of C-O, C-OH, and COO- bonds respectively [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on photophysical properties of B/N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV-Visible spectroscopy was used to explore the optical properties of B/N-CQDs prepared in aqueous solution. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of B/N-CQDs pre and post subjection to picric acid. The UV–vis absorption spectra of B/N-CQDs displayed a strong peak at 335.83 nm corresponding to n-π * transitions of CC bond due to heteroatom surface functionalities, whereas the addition of picric acid causes a bathochromic shift in the UV–vis absorption spectrum at 346.42 nm ( Fig. 5(a)). The inset shows the comparative images of B/N-CQDs in broad daylight (brown solution) and under UV-light irradiation at 365 nm (green fluorescence). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of B/N-CQDs in Fig. 5(b) shows excitation-dependent emission spectra at 340–725 nm.

The PL spectroscopy of prepared B/N-CQDs exhibits the excitation-dependent emission spectrum as observed in Fig. 6(a). The PL intensity increased linearly from 280 nm to 340 nm and gradually decreased from 350 nm due to the large particles of B/N-CQDs. The excitation spectrum is observed at 340 nm. The number of large particles which are excited in a particular wavelength has a linear relationship with PL intensity [40,43]. The area normalized excitation spectrum and the relative PL quantum yield of B/N-CQDs (24.06%) is represented in Fig. 6(b) and Fig. S1 respectively. The B/N-CQDs quantum yield was calculated by the slope method. Anthracene (0.27% in Ethanol) was used as a reference for quantum dots. The high quantum yield is mostly owing to the presence of surface functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of B/N-CQD photoluminescence stability and its application thereof in the analysis of picric acid

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence stability of the synthesized B/N-CQDs was investigated under different pH conditions and also subjecting to solutions of varying ionic strength. The photoirradiation and storage stability were also investigated. There was no stark difference in the fluorescence intensity of the B/N-CQDs on varying the ionic strength of the prepared KCl solutions (0–2 M) as seen in Fig. S2(a). The effect on fluorescence intensity of B/N-CQDs over a range of pH values from 1 to 14 is represented in Fig. S2(b). It was noticeable that the fluorescence intensity was stable in the pH range of 4–10 while the variation in fluorescence intensity was observed at extreme acidic and basic conditions. In Fig. S2(c), the optimal incubation time for.

the fluorescence intensity of B/N-CQDs was examined. There was no prominent effect in the PL intensity of B/N-CQDs under UV light at 365 nm for 60 mins. So the optimized incubation time of 60 mins was selected for the determination of stability of B/N-CQDs. Fig. S2(d), explains the long-term storage ability of B/N-CQDs for 60 days. The observed zeta potential value was found to be − 3.2 mV ( Fig. 7). This negative zeta potential value establishes the boron functionalization on the surface of CQDs [44]. The above-mentioned results conclusive evidence for the PL stability of B/N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study for the detection of toxic picric acid
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the usefulness of the proposed method, the effect of closely associated molecules on the detection of PA using B/N-CQDs has been investigated. For which 100 μL of B/N-CQDs were added to 400 μL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) at room temperature. Later 2 μL concentrations of different aromatic compounds commonly associated with PA were mixed and diluted by 2.5 mL of deionized water to obtain 16 μM concentration. The obtained solution was poured into the quartz cuvette and incubated for 2 min. The compounds used for selectivity study includes picric acid (PA), nitrobenzene (NB), aniline, chloroacetic acid (CA), catechol, benzoic acid (BA), resorcinol (Res), phenol (phe), toluene (tol), 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). Fig. 8(a) represents the selective fluorescence quenching intensity of B/N-CQDs for specific addition of PA over the other nitro-aromatic and non-nitro aromatic compounds. This can be attributed to the strong hydrogen bonding which happens between the surface of B/N-CQDs with the rich electron moieties and the hydroxyl group in PA. Fig. 8(a) confirms the selectivity of PA detection and was further confirmed by the Forster resonance electron transfer (FRET) mechanism [33]. Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the sensitivity of B/N-CQDs in selective detection of PA
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the PL intensity of B/N-CQDs with increasing concentration, different concentrations of PA (37 nM-30 μM) were added to B/N-CQDs solution. On increasing the concentration of PA, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased with a slight shift in the emission wavelength as represented in Fig. 8(b). This shift occurs due to electrostatic.

n–π * interaction due to the hydroxyl group (-OH) present in PA. A decrease in the fluorescence intensity clearly explained the quenching mechanism. The efficiency of the fluorescence quenching is linear in the range from 37 nM to 30 μM. The detection limit was calculated from the linear rage of 0–30 μM with a correlation coefficient (R2 =0.989) (Fig. 8(c)). The detection limit can be identified by using this equation,
(4) LOD = 3·3 σ/s
where ‘σ’ is the standard deviation of the sample, ‘s′ is the slope of the curve. The detection limit of PA has 1.8 nM which is better and comparable with many results as summarized in.

The PL quenching mechanism of CQDs can be explained through different processes such as (1) Reduction Electron transfer from B/N-CQDs to PA (2) Induced coordination aggregation (3) FRET (Forster resonance electron transfer) between CQDs and chromophores. The major reason for quenching of B/N-CQDs by PA is through FRET mechanism. The concept of FRET is due to dipole-dipole interactions that involve donor fluorophore which on excitation, transfer electrons to acceptor chromophore in a non-radiative way [46]. The Fluorophore acts as an oscillating dipole that exchanges energy to another dipole with the same energy in resonance. The acceptor chromophore should be non-fluorescent in FRET studies. Fig. 9(a) shows that B/N-CQDs act as a donor fluorophore and PA acts as an acceptor chromophore, which is non-fluorescent. There are some criteria for quenching mechanism which according to FRET obeys the follows (i) The absorption spectra of B/N-CQDs + PA should be overlapping with PL emission of B/N-CQDs (ii) The PL intensity should decrease while the addition of concentration of PA increases (iii) B/N-CQDs-PA must be typically close to each other [48]. To find out, the process of electron transfer, Stern-Volmer analysis has been carried out for the quenching mechanism of B/N-CQD by PA. Eq. 5 explains the Stern-Volmer equation.
(5) F0 / F = 1 + KSV [Q]
Where F and F0 describe the fluorescence intensities of B/N-CQDs in the absence and presence of PA, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Q is the concentration of PA (0–30 μM). Fig. 9(b) explains the curvature plot rather than a straight line which explains 100% static or 100% dynamic but not for both Static-dynamic quenching combinations. So, the Stern-Volmer equation has been modified to get a straight line with quenching constant (Kapp) against the concentration of PA which provides static-dynamic quenching combination, known as modified Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 9(c)). The stern-Volmer modified equation is given below,
(6) F0 / F = 1 + Kapp[Q]
Where Kapp is the modified quenching constant. For another study, to confirm the quenching mechanism, Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) studies was carried out. To determine the fluorescence lifetime of B/N-CQDs were measured using TCSPC in the presence and absence of PA (Fig. 9(d)). The results were fitted in a bi-exponential fit and the decay emission intensity was calculated (). Hence, the B/N-CQDs fluorescence lifetime is not quenched by PA, but a quenching mechanism can be observed due to the ground state interaction of B/N-CQDs with PA. The average lifetime was found to be 13.27 ns in the absence of PA and 13.11 ns in the presence of PA. This result is suggestive that on addition of PA there is a decrease in the fluorescent lifetime thereby ruling out the IFE mechanism [47].

The fluorescence lifetime reduction demonstrates the interaction between the electron-rich nitrogen atoms on the B/N-CQD and the electron-deficient nitro groups of PA ( Scheme 2). These results show that FRET is the dominant mechanism between the developed B/N-CQD and PA. Further, PA is absorbed by the synthesized B/N-CQD and forms a non-fluorescent complex through the strong electrostatic interaction between the B/N-CQD and PA. Thus, the outstanding selectivity toward PA detection is based on the FRET from B/N-CQD to PA and the formation of the nonfluorescent complex between the B/N-CQD and PA.

The cell viability and cytotoxicity assay were investigated in Hela cells using MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay as shown in Fig. 10(a). The cell viability of B/N-CQDs was greater than 97% while upon addition of B/N-CQDs with a different concentration range from 0 to 100 μg/mL (24hrs). The results confirmed that B/N-CQDs are non-toxic and bio-compatible. The synthesized B/N-CQDs proved as an excellent biological probe also confirmed by fluorescence stability and cytotoxicity.Fig. 10(b) explains the fluorescence image of B/N-CQDs using Hela cells is considered as a further application. The fluorescence images of Hela cells were incubated with B/N-CQDs. The fluorescence images were obtained by using a confocal microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of picric acid in industrial effluent water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared fluorescent B/N-CQD was investigated for detection of PA in the industrial effluent water samples. Water samples were collected from industrial effluents and filtered using Whatman filter paper. Different concentrations of picric acid were spiked into the solution and an aliquot of the solution was analysed for picric acid by using the proposed procedure. The obtained recovery values were found to be in the range of 98–101%. The results of the analysis of industrial effluents were compared statistically by the t-test and by the variance ratio F-test with those obtained by the reference method. The t-values the variance ratio F-values did not exceed the theoretical values, indicating that there was no significant difference in accuracy and precision between proposed and reference methods. Table 2 summarizes the analysis of PA in industrial effluents.

The present work employs simple microwave irradiation treatment to make highly luminous, water-soluble, multi-functionalized CQDs from L-serine, citric acid, and boric acid, which is rapid and energy-efficient. The developed B/N-CQDs are readily soluble in an aqueous medium, have a high PL QY, a long shelf life in solution, and have great cell viability. The B/N-CDs displayed excellent photostability and remained unaffected over a wide range of pH and ionic strength. The B/N-CQD showed strong green fluorescence under UV light at 360 nm. The synthesized CQDs displayed outstanding sensing ability towards PA in the industrial effluent water samples. Low LOD, rapid response, and a broad linear range of sensing are the main advantages of B/N-CQDs when compared to conventional methods.

Keerthana P: Conceptualization, Methodology. Anila Rose Cherian: Conceptualization, Methodology. Uraiwan Sirimahachai: Characterization, Data interpretation. Ditto Abraham Thadathil: Investigation. Anitha Varghese: Writing – review & editing. Gurumurthy Hegde: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The researchers and authors express their gratitude to the Major Research Project (Major Research Project DSC-1830), Center for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka, India for providing the required financial support for completing this research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2022.107209.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of boron nitride quantum dots/poly(luminol) nanocomposite for selective detection of ascorbic acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Jerome, A.K. Sundramoorthy, Hydrothermal Synthesis of Boron Nitride Quantum Dots/Poly(Luminol) Nanocomposite for Selective Detection of Ascorbic Acid, J. Electrochem. Soc. 166 (2019) B3017–B3024. https://doi.org/10.1149/2.0041909jes.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of fluorescent hydrophobic carbon quantum dots and their use for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Cheng, X. An, C. Zheng, S. Cao, Green synthesis of fluorescent hydrophobic carbon quantum dots and their use for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection, RSC Adv. 5 (2015) 93360–93363. https://doi.org/10.1039/c5ra19029k.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S.A. Hussain, An Introduction to Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), 2009. 〈http://arxiv.org/abs/0908.1815〉.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile one step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots for label -free fluorescence sensing approach to detect picric acid in aqueous solution
